name: Nash Equilibrium Resettlement Matching
executive_summary:
The Nash Equilibrium Resettlement Matching framework applies two-sided stable matching theory -- specifically the Gale-Shapley deferred acceptance algorithm and its Nash equilibrium properties -- to refugee resettlement allocation. Current resettlement systems in most countries operate through administrative assignment: a central authority (UNHCR referral, national resettlement agency) assigns refugee families to receiving communities based on available slots, processing capacity, and rough demographic matching. This approach treats resettlement as a one-sided allocation problem, ignoring the preferences and local knowledge of both refugees and receiving municipalities. The result is high rates of secondary migration (refugees relocating from assigned communities to preferred ones within 6-18 months), wasted integration resources in communities that prepared for arrivals who subsequently left, and suboptimal integration outcomes driven by mismatches between refugee needs (language community, employment sector, family proximity, cultural infrastructure) and community resources.

The framework reconceptualizes resettlement as a two-sided matching market. Refugee families rank available destinations based on factors that predict integration success: existing co-ethnic or co-linguistic community size, employment opportunities matching their skills, proximity to extended family members already resettled, educational resources for children, and climate similarity to country of origin. Receiving municipalities rank refugee profiles based on their integration resource fit: available housing stock matching family size, local labor market gaps the refugees could fill, language training capacity, school enrollment capacity, and community volunteer support infrastructure. The Gale-Shapley deferred acceptance algorithm produces a stable matching -- an assignment where no refugee-municipality pair would mutually prefer each other over their current assignment. This stability property is the Nash equilibrium: no individual actor (refugee or municipality) can improve their outcome by unilateral deviation from the assigned match.

The framework's practical prediction is that stable matching with bilateral preference elicitation will reduce secondary migration, improve integration metrics (employment rates, language acquisition, social participation), and increase municipal satisfaction with resettlement outcomes. The mechanism is not merely better assignment but incentive compatibility: when participants know their preferences are incorporated, they are more likely to invest in making the assigned placement work rather than planning to relocate. The boundary condition is that stable matching requires genuine choice on both sides -- it cannot function when receiving communities are coerced into participation or when refugee families have no meaningful variation in available options. It also requires preference elicitation infrastructure that may be difficult to implement in crisis-tempo resettlement scenarios where processing speed matters more than match quality.

mapped_components.unit: A single refugee-family-to-municipality assignment: one family matched to one receiving community. Each assignment has a bilateral quality score determined by how well the family's needs align with the municipality's resources and how well the municipality's integration capacity fits the family's profile. The assignment is the atomic unit on which stability is evaluated.
mapped_components.variation: The diversity of preferences across both sides of the market. Refugee families vary in language, skills, family network geography, cultural needs, and climate adaptation. Municipalities vary in housing availability, labor market gaps, language training capacity, school capacity, and community support infrastructure. This bilateral variation creates the matching problem: if all families and municipalities were identical, any random assignment would be equivalent.
mapped_components.selection: The Gale-Shapley deferred acceptance algorithm: families propose to their most-preferred municipality; municipalities tentatively accept their most-preferred applicants up to capacity, rejecting others; rejected families propose to their next choice. The algorithm iterates until all families are matched. Selection pressure acts on both sides simultaneously, producing a stable matching through iterative preference revelation.
mapped_components.fitness: Assignment fitness is measured by integration outcomes: 12-month employment rate, language proficiency gains, school enrollment and attendance for children, self-reported wellbeing, and -- most directly -- the absence of secondary migration. A high-fitness assignment is one where both the family and the municipality are sufficiently satisfied that neither seeks to deviate from the match.
mapped_components.boundary: The framework requires: (a) multiple receiving communities with differentiated resource profiles (no matching problem if all destinations are identical), (b) meaningful preference variation among refugee families (no matching problem if all families want the same destination), (c) sufficient resettlement volume that municipalities receive multiple potential matches (the algorithm cannot function with one-to-one forced assignment), and (d) processing timelines that accommodate preference elicitation (incompatible with emergency mass displacement where speed dominates). It also assumes that expressed preferences predict integration success, which may not hold for families with limited information about destination options.

falsifiable_predictions: Resettlement programs implementing two-sided stable matching with preference elicitation from both refugees and municipalities will show 12-month secondary migration rates at least 30% lower than administrative assignment programs within the same country, controlling for refugee demographics, resettlement volume, and municipal characteristics.

Refugees matched through preference-incorporating algorithms will show higher 12-month employment rates (5-15 percentage points) than administratively assigned refugees, because matching incorporates labor market fit information that administrative assignment ignores.

Municipalities participating in preference-based matching will report higher satisfaction with resettlement outcomes (measured by willingness to accept future refugees and council/community survey scores) than municipalities receiving administratively assigned refugees, because the matching process incorporates their capacity constraints and preferences.

The stable matching will exhibit the proposer-advantage property documented in Gale-Shapley theory: the side that proposes (families, in the refugee-proposing variant) will receive systematically better matches (closer to top-ranked preferences) than the receiving side (municipalities). Switching to a municipality-proposing variant will reverse this advantage, providing a natural experiment for testing the algorithm's theoretical properties in practice.

Secondary migration patterns in current administrative-assignment systems will cluster at destinations that are highly ranked by refugees sharing the migrants' characteristics (co-ethnic community, employment sector, language), confirming that refugee preferences are real, consistent, and predictive of actual relocation behavior -- the prerequisite for matching-based assignment to improve outcomes.

Preference elicitation will reveal that refugee families' destination rankings correlate more strongly with co-ethnic community size and family proximity (R > 0.5) than with per-capita income or general quality-of-life indices of the destination, suggesting that administrative systems that optimize for economic indicators are optimizing for the wrong variables.

supporting_evidence.finding: Gale and Shapley (1962) proved that the deferred acceptance algorithm produces a stable matching for any two-sided market with strict preferences, and that the resulting matching is optimal for the proposing side. This result, extended by Roth (1984) to show strategy-proofness on the proposing side, earned Shapley and Roth the 2012 Nobel Prize in Economics.
supporting_evidence.relevance: Provides the theoretical foundation: the algorithm's existence, stability guarantee, and incentive compatibility are proven mathematical results, not empirical approximations. This means the framework's core mechanism (stable matching reduces secondary migration by eliminating mutually beneficial deviations) is guaranteed by theory under the model's assumptions.
supporting_evidence.source: Gale, D. and Shapley, L. (1962). College Admissions and the Stability of Marriage. American Mathematical Monthly, 69(1), 9-15.

supporting_evidence.finding: The National Resident Matching Program (NRMP) has used the Gale-Shapley algorithm to match medical school graduates to residency programs in the United States since 1952. Analysis by Roth (1984, 2002) showed that the matching market stabilized after adoption of the algorithm: participation rates increased, post-match reneging decreased, and geographic redistribution of residents improved relative to the uncoordinated pre-algorithm market.
supporting_evidence.relevance: Demonstrates that stable matching works at scale in a real two-sided market with heterogeneous participants, preference diversity, and significant stakes. The NRMP's success over 70+ years provides existence proof that the mechanism can sustainably coordinate large-scale allocation with bilateral preferences.
supporting_evidence.source: Roth, A.E. (2002). The Economist as Engineer: Game Theory, Experimentation, and Computation as Tools for Design Economics. Econometrica, 70(4), 1341-1378.

supporting_evidence.finding: Bansak et al. (2018) used machine learning to predict refugee employment outcomes as a function of refugee characteristics and destination features, then optimized assignment to maximize predicted employment. Their algorithm improved predicted employment rates by 40-70% compared to the status quo in the US and Switzerland. While not a full two-sided matching, this work demonstrated that data-driven assignment incorporating destination-specific factors substantially outperforms administrative assignment.
supporting_evidence.relevance: Provides direct evidence that optimization-based refugee assignment outperforms administrative assignment. The current framework extends Bansak et al. by incorporating bilateral preferences (not just predicted outcomes from one side) and by using stability rather than aggregate optimization as the objective, which provides individual-level incentive compatibility that aggregate optimization does not.
supporting_evidence.source: Bansak, K. et al. (2018). Improving Refugee Integration through Data-Driven Algorithmic Assignment. Science, 359(6373), 325-329.

supporting_evidence.finding: Damm (2009) studied Danish refugee resettlement and found that administrative random assignment to municipalities led to significant secondary migration within 2-5 years, with refugees relocating toward larger co-ethnic communities and better-matched labor markets. Secondary migration improved employment outcomes, suggesting that the original assignment was suboptimal from the refugees' perspective and that revealed preferences contain integration-relevant information.
supporting_evidence.relevance: Demonstrates both the problem (administrative assignment generates high secondary migration) and the mechanism (secondary migration follows preference-predictable patterns). The finding that secondary migration improves outcomes confirms that refugee preferences are informative about integration success, validating the premise that incorporating preferences into initial assignment would reduce costly post-assignment relocation.
supporting_evidence.source: Damm, A.P. (2009). Ethnic Enclaves and Immigrant Labor Market Outcomes: Quasi-Experimental Evidence. Journal of Labor Economics, 27(2), 281-314.

experimental_designs.name: Pilot Two-Sided Matching Resettlement Trial
experimental_designs.description: Partner with a national resettlement agency (e.g., UNHCR country office, national refugee council) to implement a pilot two-sided matching program for a subset of refugee arrivals over 18-24 months. Participating refugee families (treatment group, n=200-400 families) complete a structured preference elicitation survey ranking available municipalities on key dimensions (language community, employment sector, family proximity, climate, urban/rural preference). Participating municipalities (10-20 communities) complete a parallel survey describing their integration resources and ranking refugee profiles they can best serve. A Gale-Shapley algorithm (refugee-proposing) produces the stable matching. The control group (n=200-400 families) is assigned through the existing administrative process to the same set of municipalities. Primary outcomes measured at 6, 12, and 24 months: secondary migration rate, employment rate, language proficiency (standardized test), children's school attendance, and self-reported wellbeing (WHO-5). Secondary outcomes: municipal integration resource utilization efficiency and community attitudes toward refugees.
experimental_designs.hypothesis: The matching group will show 30-50% lower 12-month secondary migration rates, 8-15 percentage point higher employment rates, and higher self-reported wellbeing scores compared to the administrative-assignment control group. Effects will be strongest for families with clearly differentiated preferences (e.g., rare language communities, specialized skills) and weakest for families with homogeneous or weakly held preferences.
experimental_designs.feasibility: Moderate-to-challenging. Requires government or UNHCR partnership for program access, which is the primary feasibility bottleneck. Preference elicitation infrastructure (multilingual surveys, interviewer training) requires development. The randomization requirement may face ethical objections: denying preference incorporation to the control group when it is available for the treatment group raises equity concerns. A stepped-wedge design (phased rollout across municipalities) may be more politically feasible than simultaneous randomization. Sample size requirements are substantial: detecting a 10 percentage point employment difference with 80% power requires approximately 250 families per arm.

experimental_designs.name: Retrospective Preference Reconstruction Analysis
experimental_designs.description: Using existing resettlement records from a country with administrative assignment (e.g., Sweden, Germany, or the US), reconstruct revealed preferences from secondary migration patterns. For each refugee family that relocated within 24 months, identify the destination they moved to and code the characteristics that differentiate it from their original assignment (co-ethnic community size, employment sector match, family proximity, housing cost, school quality). For families that did not relocate, code the match quality of their original assignment on the same dimensions. Test whether a Gale-Shapley matching using reconstructed preferences would have assigned the secondary migrants to destinations closer to their revealed preferences in the first place, and estimate the reduction in secondary migration that preference-incorporating assignment would have achieved.
experimental_designs.hypothesis: At least 60% of secondary migration events would be predicted (the family's actual destination would rank higher in their reconstructed preference list than their original assignment). A counterfactual Gale-Shapley matching using reconstructed preferences would reduce predicted secondary migration by 40-60%. The strongest predictors of secondary migration direction will be co-ethnic community size at destination and family proximity, outweighing economic indicators as predictors.
experimental_designs.feasibility: High, given data access. National resettlement databases in Sweden (Migrationsverket), Germany (BAMF), and the US (State Department/ORR) contain initial placement and subsequent address information. The main challenge is data access authorization, which typically requires institutional research partnership. The preference reconstruction methodology requires assumptions about revealed preference (that migration direction reveals preference), which may be confounded by information availability and moving costs.

limitations: Stable matching assumes that preferences are well-formed, consistent, and informed. Refugee families fleeing crisis situations may have limited information about destination options, leading to poorly calibrated preferences that do not predict actual integration satisfaction. Preference elicitation conducted in transit camps or processing centers may reflect immediate emotional states rather than stable, considered judgments about long-term living arrangements.

The framework treats municipalities as unitary actors with coherent preferences, but municipal decision-making involves multiple stakeholders (elected officials, housing authorities, school boards, community organizations) with potentially conflicting objectives. A municipality's stated preferences may reflect political considerations rather than genuine integration capacity, undermining the matching quality.

Gale-Shapley is strategy-proof only on the proposing side. If municipalities propose, refugee families have incentive to misrepresent preferences; if refugees propose, municipalities have incentive to strategically rank profiles. In practice, strategic behavior may be limited by cognitive complexity, but sophisticated actors (well-resourced municipalities with dedicated resettlement staff) may game the system to their advantage.

The framework may inadvertently reinforce ethnic concentration. If refugees strongly prefer destinations with existing co-ethnic communities and municipalities prefer refugees who match existing demographic profiles, the matching algorithm will produce ethnically concentrated assignments. Whether ethnic concentration helps or hinders long-term integration is empirically contested (enclave effects can be both resource-providing and mobility-limiting).

Scaling the preference elicitation process to crisis-tempo resettlement (thousands of families per month during mass displacement events) may be operationally infeasible. The infrastructure required for multilingual preference surveys, municipality capacity assessments, and algorithmic matching adds processing time that conflicts with the urgency of protection needs. The framework may be best suited for planned, quota-based resettlement rather than emergency response.

The stability concept (no mutually preferred deviation) does not imply optimality. A stable matching may leave significant welfare gains unrealized if they require coordinated multi-party swaps that no bilateral deviation achieves. The framework guarantees no individual defection incentive but not that the overall assignment maximizes aggregate integration outcomes.

connections_to_other_frameworks.framework: Loss Aversion Reference Point Calibration Model
connections_to_other_frameworks.relationship: Both apply formal decision-science frameworks to emotionally charged allocation decisions. The veterinary framework addresses individual-level cognitive bias in treatment choice; the resettlement framework addresses market-level allocation failure in placement. Both predict that structured decision architectures incorporating participant preferences outperform ad hoc or authority-directed allocation.

Stable matching produces assignments that function as focal points for post-placement behavior: because both parties participated in the matching and neither has a unilateral deviation incentive, the assignment becomes a coordination anchor that both sides invest in maintaining. Administrative assignment lacks this focal-point quality, contributing to the instability that drives secondary migration.

Both frameworks argue for differentiated rather than uniform policy approaches. OVPF differentiates pandemic restrictions by venue risk profile; the resettlement framework differentiates placement by bilateral preference fit. Both predict that uniform policies (blanket lockdowns, administrative assignment) waste resources on low-impact interventions while underserving the areas where targeted action would yield the greatest returns.

Both frameworks achieve coordination without central authority. Firefly synchronization achieves temporal coordination through local pulse coupling; stable matching achieves allocation coordination through preference revelation. Both demonstrate that decentralized mechanisms can produce globally coherent outcomes, though the mechanisms differ: pulse coupling is iterative and continuous, while Gale-Shapley is a one-shot algorithm producing a fixed assignment.
